content:
4 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Photo by Atharva Tulsi on UnsplashI am a 20 year old living in the country’s capital with just about every privilege I would need to live comfortably. And yet, I am plagued by a sense of overwhelm I’ve never felt before. I’d prefer to be blissfully ignorant about the state of the world right now, but my anxiety wouldn’t let me off the hook.
As a ritual participant in doom-scrolling, my algorithm consisted of pointless videos about nothing, until recently, when all I’m being shown are reels upon reels of suppressed voices, destruction, mining, and corruption under the guise of development. This isn’t the best sort of Instagram feed for someone who’s brought to tears looking at fish. As an Earth-dweller, I owe it to my fellow creatures to listen when they need me to, as well as to do everything in my power. I’ve been told the written word is a weapon, therefore, I must use mine.
When I was younger, the doom brought on by climate change was always supposed to take over far into the future. I pictured it as a permanent reliance on oxygen masks and plants grown in artificial pods in an almost cyberpunk-esque world, assuming we’d get to that point. It wasn’t supposed to happen as I’m yet to even experience the world. To be fair, I was immature, and you could blame me for not questioning the almost opaque smog on the way to school on a November morning. Whatever bubble I was in burst long ago, but what drove me to begin writing is a recent event.
My neighbourhood, just as many others, has been receiving decreasing amounts of water for a few weeks now, and nobody seems enraged enough. I was, and was told that it is just because it’s too hot in my city. It won’t be long before people adapt to it and find ways to live with it, instead of questioning it. This isn’t a new issue, it is something people in lesser privileged parts of the country have been facing for a long time without respite, but we go about our lives ignoring what doesn’t bother us, until it does. We’re gradually losing the resource responsible for our survival to pure ignorance. To add insult to injury, the new AI data centres are going to put even more pressure on already water-stressed regions, while the people living far away from them are engulfed in 15 second clips of a samosa fighting a jalebi.
If you thought the seas and the islands were safe, you’re wrong. The Greater Nicobar Project, aiming to develop an International Container Transshipment Terminal, Greenfield International Airport, Township and Area Development, and a power plant in the dense forest-rich Greater Nicobar Island, is expected to “divert” close to a million trees, while also endangering the local Shompen and the Nicobarese Tribes. I find the official use of the word “divert” especially amusing since the trees are almost certainly going to be flattened in the process. The flora of the region, which is a UNESCO biosphere reserve, comprises 13.11% species that are endemic to the island, that is, they can’t be found anywhere else. In terms of fauna, there are over 1800 species on the island, which is why I’m greatly struggling to understand how the Nicobar long-tailed macaques or the giant leatherback sea turtles are going to benefit from the compensatory forest going to be planted in Haryana (if it even is).
The Shompen tribe is a Particularly Vulnerable Tribal Group (PVTG). They’ve inhabited the island for tens of thousands of years and they practice animism, i.e., nature worship. They actively protect and nurture even the tiniest of shrubs and yet no thought has been given to their chances of survival, because in this new era, “strategy” somehow overrides humanity. Just the introduction of foreign germs to the Shompen tribe that hasn’t developed immunity to it may have deadly consequences.
I’ve seen people my age claiming it’s the environmentalists that obstruct any growth in the country, which makes me wonder at the level of detachment one would have to practice to separate themselves from what enables their very existence, nature. How this indifference to our surroundings exists is absurd to me.
This is just the tip of the iceberg. Throughout the country trees are being felled at a large scale, hundreds upon thousands of years of ecological history gone with them. Ancient hills, billions of years old, are being razed for mining, which is something I witnessed myself from afar on my trip to Rajasthan and was a greatly unnerving sight. People would gladly be raving about thousands of years old heritage but not nearly as much about equally old ecosystems.
This is an ecological disaster waiting to happen and if anyone is inclined to believe it even remotely has anything to do with politics then they are mistaken. The problem is and always was an economic system built on capitalism that only works to favour those that benefit from the endless destruction of our planet, not you or me. Humans have only been around for about 300,000 years, a fraction of our predecessors, and yet we have managed to cause more harm than all of them combined. My generation, which is going to have to bear the brunt of it all, is unjustifiably shamed for giving in to algorithms and brain-rot that was designed to do exactly that, while we’re constantly surrounded by a form of climate anxiety unknown to the ones before us.
The systems that claimed to support us have only actively engaged in worsening the state of affairs. When you rob a people of their rights to their planet, you don’t wonder at the loss of hope among them. One question remains: who is the “development” going to benefit if there’s no one left to save?
